content:
Practitioners have long distinguished **too-simple** fits from **too-wiggly** ones. **Polynomial regression** demos from the 20th century showed low-degree curves underfitting and high-degree curves chasing noise — the visual language still used in courses today.

**Cross-validation** (Stone, 1974; later k-fold refinements) made **out-of-sample error** the standard selection criterion, formalizing the idea that training performance alone is misleading when models overfit.

**Neural network** training in the 1990s highlighted **early stopping** — halt when validation error rises even as training error falls — as a practical overfitting remedy before modern regularization stacks.

**Hastie, Tibshirani, and Friedman's** *Elements of Statistical Learning* (2001) unified **learning curves**, **validation curves**, and the **bias–variance** picture for choosing model complexity.

Modern **deep learning** revived the same tension at scale: huge models can underfit if under-trained or overfit small fine-tuning sets — diagnostics still rest on **train vs validation** behavior, early stopping, and regularization.

timeline.label: Polynomial curve fitting (classical statistics)
timeline.description: Visual underfitting (low degree) vs overfitting (high degree) established intuitive language for model flexibility.

timeline.label: Cross-validation for model selection (1970s)
timeline.description: Held-out error estimation becomes standard for detecting overfitting during hyperparameter choice.

timeline.label: Early stopping in neural nets (1990s)
timeline.description: Validation-based stopping prevents late-training overfitting when training loss still decreases.

timeline.label: ESL learning & validation curves (2001)
timeline.description: Textbook treatment links complexity knobs and sample size to under/overfitting regimes.

timeline.label: Large-scale deep learning (2010s–present)
timeline.description: Same train/val diagnostics guide pretraining, fine-tuning, and regularization at billion-parameter scale.
